title: Sri Lankan Pol Sambol

ingredients.name: freshly grated coconut
visual_checks: Freshly grated gives the best texture; frozen grated coconut (thawed) is a reliable second choice.
ingredients.notes: Freshly grated gives the best texture; frozen grated coconut (thawed) is a reliable second choice.

subs.name: 3 tbsp desiccated coconut soaked in 2 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes — the texture is slightly drier but the flavour is close.

ingredients.name: Maldive fish (umbalakada / dried tuna flakes)
visual_checks: Dried cured tuna with a deep umami punch. Omit entirely for a vegan version — the relish is still delicious.
ingredients.notes: Dried cured tuna with a deep umami punch. Omit entirely for a vegan version — the relish is still delicious.

subs.name: Omit entirely for a vegan version; the coconut, chilli, and lime combination stands on its own and is widely eaten across Sri Lanka.

ingredients.name: dried red chillies, broken
visual_checks: Adjust quantity to taste; these provide the backbone heat.
ingredients.notes: Adjust quantity to taste; these provide the backbone heat.

ingredients.name: lime juice
visual_checks: Lime is traditional; lemon juice works equally well.
ingredients.notes: Lime is traditional; lemon juice works equally well.

subs.name: 1 tsp white vinegar or 1 tbsp tamarind water provides the necessary sourness if lime is unavailable.

steps.text: If using Maldive fish, soak the flakes in 2 tbsp warm water for 5 minutes, then drain and break them apart into smaller pieces. This softens the dried fish and tempers its intense saltiness.
Combine the grated coconut, soaked Maldive fish (if using), red chilli powder, and broken dried red chillies in a large bowl. Add the finely chopped red onion and lime juice.

Using your fingertips, work the mixture together by rubbing and squeezing — you want the coconut to absorb the red colour and become lightly stained from the chilli. Alternatively, pulse everything 3–4 times in a mixie, stopping before it becomes a paste; the coconut should retain some texture.

Add salt, taste, and adjust for heat, sourness, and seasoning. Let the sambol rest for 5 minutes before serving so the coconut absorbs the lime and chilli. Serve alongside rice and curry, hoppers, or roti.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → Omit entirely for a vegan version; the coconut, chilli, and lime combination stands on its own and is widely eaten across Sri Lanka.; coconut → 3 tbsp desiccated coconut soaked in 2 tbsp warm water for 10 minutes — the texture is slightly drier but the flavour is close.; lemon → 1 tsp white vinegar or 1 tbsp tamarind water provides the necessary sourness if lime is unavailable.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Fresh grated coconut is widely available in South India; use frozen grated coconut (thawed) anywhere else. The dry Maldive fish has no exact Indian equivalent — a small piece of dried salted fish (Bombay duck / bombil) crumbled in gives a similar umami depth, or simply omit for a clean vegan relish. Prepare in a mixie with the pulse function rather than a stone grinder.
